name: Command and Scripting Interpreter
description: Adversaries may abuse command and script interpreters to execute commands, scripts, or binaries. Sub-techniques include PowerShell, Bash, Python, JavaScript, Visual Basic.

name: Phishing
description: Adversaries send phishing messages with malicious attachments or links. Sub-techniques: spearphishing attachment, link, service.

name: Valid Accounts
tactic: Initial Access / Persistence / Privilege Escalation / Defense Evasion
description: Adversaries use legitimate account credentials to gain access. Often the most common, hardest-to-detect technique.

name: Exploit Public-Facing Application
description: Exploit a vulnerability in an internet-facing application to gain initial access.

name: Server Software Component
description: Web shells, IIS components, transport agents, SQL stored procedures, terminal services DLLs.

name: Boot or Logon Autostart Execution
description: Configure system settings to automatically execute a program during boot or logon. Includes registry run keys, scheduled tasks at logon, LSASS drivers.

name: OS Credential Dumping
description: Dump credentials from memory (LSASS), the registry, or files (SAM, Kerberos tickets, NTDS.dit).

name: Brute Force
description: Password guessing, password spraying, credential stuffing.

name: Remote System Discovery
description: Identify other systems on the network. net view, ping sweeps, port scans, AD enumeration.

name: File and Directory Discovery
description: Enumerate files and directories on the system or network shares to find sensitive content.

name: Remote Services
description: Use remote services (RDP, SMB, SSH, WinRM, VNC) to move laterally.

name: Data Encrypted for Impact
description: Encrypt data to render it inaccessible — ransomware.

name: Exfiltration Over Web Service
description: Exfiltrate data over web services like cloud storage (Dropbox, Google Drive), code hosting (GitHub), or pastebins.

name: Exfiltration Over C2 Channel
description: Steal data over the existing command-and-control channel.

name: Obfuscated Files or Information
description: Encrypted, encoded, or compressed files; steganography; software packing.

name: Process Injection
description: Inject code into another process: DLL injection, thread execution hijacking, process hollowing, APC injection.

name: Application Layer Protocol
description: Use legitimate application-layer protocols (HTTP, DNS, mail) to blend in with normal traffic.

name: Data Encoding
description: Encode C2 traffic using standard or non-standard encoding to evade simple detection.

name: Encrypted Channel
description: Use TLS or other crypto for C2 to evade content inspection.

name: Proxy
description: Use proxies — internal, external, multi-hop, domain fronting — to hide C2 origin.

name: Account Manipulation
description: Modify account properties (group membership, additional credentials, SSH keys) to maintain access.

name: Create Account
description: Create local, domain, or cloud accounts to maintain access.

name: Modify Authentication Process
description: Modify password filters, PAM, hybrid identity, conditional access policies to capture credentials or bypass MFA.

name: Steal Web Session Cookie
description: Steal browser session cookies to bypass MFA and authenticate as the victim.

name: Exploit — SSRF
description: Server-Side Request Forgery in a public-facing app pivots to internal services.

name: Python
description: Execute commands or payloads via Python interpreter.

name: PowerShell
description: Execute commands via PowerShell — heavily abused, well-instrumented in modern Windows.

name: Virtualization/Sandbox Evasion
description: Detect sandbox environments (timing, mouse activity, hardware properties) and refuse to run.

name: Gather Victim Host Information
description: Banner grabs, software versions, OS, network configuration, public services.

name: Gather Victim Identity Information
description: Email addresses, employee names, credentials in public breaches.

name: Exfiltration to Cloud Storage
description: Upload stolen data to Dropbox, Google Drive, OneDrive, Mega, etc.
